name: Kami of Water
description: In Japanese mythology, the Kami of Water encompasses a wide range of deities associated with various aspects of water, from the vast oceans and rushing rivers to tranquil lakes and life-giving rain. There isn't a single, monolithic 'Kami of Water,' but rather a collection of individual kami who preside over specific bodies of water or water-related phenomena. These kami are crucial to agriculture, sustenance, and purification rituals within Japanese culture. Some of the most prominent water kami include Suijin, often considered the general deity of water and associated with irrigation and agriculture; Mizuha-no-me, a goddess of water birth and the source of water; and various dragon kami (Ryujin), who control the seas and rainfall. Their powers extend to controlling the flow of water, summoning rain, preventing floods, and ensuring the fertility of the land. Offerings are often made to these kami to ensure a bountiful harvest, safe travels on water, and protection from water-related disasters. Respect for water and its life-giving properties is deeply ingrained in Japanese culture, reflecting the importance of these water kami in the spiritual and practical lives of the people. Different regions and communities may have their own local water kami with unique stories and attributes, further enriching the tapestry of Japanese water mythology. The Kami of Water are not always benevolent; they can be wrathful if disrespected or neglected, causing droughts, floods, or other calamities. Therefore, proper veneration and respect are essential for maintaining harmony with these powerful entities.
subtitle: Guardians of the Life-Giving Waters

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Mizuha-no-me
keyMyths.description: Mizuha-no-me is born from the excrement of Izanami after she suffered burns creating Kagutsuchi, the fire kami. This highlights water's role in balancing fire and sustaining life.

keyMyths.title: Ryujin's Wrath
keyMyths.description: Ryujin, the dragon kami of the sea, can cause storms and tsunamis when angered. Stories often involve appeasing him with offerings to calm the seas.

keyMyths.title: Suijin and Irrigation
keyMyths.description: Suijin is often invoked for successful irrigation and rice cultivation. Farmers pray to Suijin for plentiful rain and protection from floods.
